title: PPI: blocks the H+/K+ ATPase (final common step)
vignette: A new medication, drug A, is being developed for the treatment of peptic ulcer disease. During initial testing, gastric acid secretion in experimental animals is stimulated by vagal stimulation, histamine infusion, or gastrin infusion. Before the stimulus is administered, the animals are divided into 2 groups, one group receives pretreatment with drug A, and the second one serves as the control and is given an injection of an equivalent volume of normal saline. The effects of the drug on hydrochloric acid (HCl) output by the stomach mucosa are recorded and shown in the graphs. Drug A reduces HCl output in response to all three stimuli (vagal, histamine, and gastrin). Drug A is most similar to which of the following medications?

clues.text: stimulated by vagal stimulation, histamine infusion, or gastrin infusion
clues.note: Three independent stimuli of parietal-cell acid secretion: vagus (ACh on M3), histamine (H2), and gastrin (CCK-B). A drug that blocks only one would leave the other two intact.

clues.text: reduces HCl output in response to all three stimuli
clues.note: If the drug blunts acid from ALL THREE pathways, it must act at the FINAL COMMON step — the H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) on the parietal cell apical membrane. That's a proton pump inhibitor (PPI).

whatMatters: Drug A reduces acid no matter which upstream stimulus (vagal/histamine/gastrin) is used, so it must act at the shared downstream effector — the H+/K+ ATPase. That makes it a proton pump inhibitor, most similar to lansoprazole. Atropine (M3) and cimetidine (H2) each block only ONE pathway, so neither could suppress all three.
strategy: When a drug blocks an output despite MULTIPLE upstream stimuli, it acts at the final common pathway. For parietal cells, the three inputs (ACh/M3, histamine/H2, gastrin/CCK-B) converge on the H+/K+ ATPase — so 'blocks all three' = PPI.

eliminations.label: atropine — blocks only the vagal (M3) input (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: atropine is a muscarinic (M3) antagonist; it blocks the cholinergic/vagal stimulation of acid. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it would reduce only the VAGAL-stimulated acid, leaving histamine- and gastrin-driven secretion intact — so it can't suppress all three. ✅ Correct if: only the vagal/cholinergic stimulus were blunted.

eliminations.label: bethanechol — a cholinergic AGONIST (increases acid)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: bethanechol is a muscarinic agonist (used for urinary retention). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it would INCREASE, not decrease, acid secretion — opposite effect. ✅ Correct if: the experiment sought a drug that stimulates secretion/motility.

eliminations.label: cimetidine — blocks only the histamine (H2) input
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: cimetidine is an H2-receptor antagonist; it blunts histamine-stimulated acid (and partly the potentiation of the other pathways). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it primarily blocks the HISTAMINE arm, so vagal- and gastrin-driven secretion persist substantially — it can't equally abolish all three like a PPI. ✅ Correct if: only the histamine-stimulated acid were reduced.

eliminations.label: sucralfate — mucosal barrier, doesn't block secretion
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: sucralfate forms a protective gel over ulcer bases (cytoprotection); it doesn't inhibit acid production. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it wouldn't reduce HCl output in any of the stimulation graphs because it acts on the mucosa, not the parietal cell pump. ✅ Correct if: the drug were meant to coat/protect the ulcer rather than reduce acid.

reasoning: Three upstream stimuli (vagal/M3, histamine/H2, gastrin/CCK-B) drive parietal-cell acid
Drug A reduces acid from ALL three → it acts at the final common step
The shared effector is the H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump)
Proton pump inhibitor = lansoprazole → answer D
mechanism: Parietal-cell acid secretion is driven by three receptors — M3 (acetylcholine/vagus), H2 (histamine from ECL cells), and CCK-B (gastrin) — that all converge to activate the apical H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump). Proton pump inhibitors (lansoprazole, omeprazole) irreversibly inhibit this pump, the final common effector, abolishing acid output regardless of which upstream stimulus is applied. Receptor-specific blockers (atropine for M3, cimetidine for H2) only affect their single pathway.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Pharm/GI > gastric acid regulation and PUD drugs: the parietal-cell diagram (M3, H2, CCK-B → H+/K+ ATPase) and drug classes (PPIs, H2 blockers, antimuscarinics, sucralfate, misoprostol).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Use the experiment's logic: blocking ALL stimuli = acting at the convergence point (the proton pump). Identify the final common step, then name the PPI.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on H+/K+ ATPase/proton pump, M3 vs H2 vs CCK-B receptors, PPI vs H2 blocker, parietal/ECL cells? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Atropine (M3) and cimetidine (H2) each block ONE input and tempt you — but only a proton pump inhibitor can suppress acid from all three stimuli. 'Blocks all three' = final-step PPI.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference is the parietal-cell acid-secretion schematic (vagus/ACh-M3, gastrin-CCKB, histamine-H2 → ATPase). Trace the three inputs converging on the pump to see why only a PPI blocks them all.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.front: Drug blocking acid from vagal + histamine + gastrin
keywords.back: Proton pump inhibitor (acts on H+/K+ ATPase)

keywords.front: Final common step of acid secretion

firstAid.detail: Pharm > GI: PPIs (irreversibly inhibit H+/K+ ATPase), uses (PUD, GERD, H. pylori), side effects (B12/Mg/Ca, infection risk).
firstAid.location: Pharm > GI: PPIs (irreversibly inhibit H+/K+ ATPase), uses (PUD, GERD, H. pylori), side effects (B12/Mg/Ca, infection risk).

firstAid.detail: GI/Physiology > parietal-cell regulation: ACh/M3, gastrin/CCK-B, histamine/H2 converging on the proton pump; ECL cells, somatostatin.
firstAid.location: GI/Physiology > parietal-cell regulation: ACh/M3, gastrin/CCK-B, histamine/H2 converging on the proton pump; ECL cells, somatostatin.

firstAid.detail: Pharm > H2 blockers (cimetidine), antimuscarinics, sucralfate, misoprostol, antacids — mechanisms and limits.
firstAid.location: Pharm > H2 blockers (cimetidine), antimuscarinics, sucralfate, misoprostol, antacids — mechanisms and limits.

memoryHook: Block the PUMP, block them ALL: vagus, histamine, gastrin all funnel into the H+/K+ ATPase — only a PPI shuts every tap at once.
